 {"id": "franken_networkx-1p2", "title": "Generators: Stochastic block models and planted partition", "description": "Generators: Stochastic Block Models

Community-structured random graph generators based on stochastic block model theory.

## Functions to Implement

- `stochastic_block_model(sizes, p, nodelist=None, seed=None, directed=False, selfloops=False, sparse=True) -> Graph`: Generate SBM with `sizes` (list of community sizes) and `p` (k×k probability matrix). Each edge (u,v) exists with probability p[community(u)][community(v)].
- `planted_partition_graph(l, k, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) -> Graph`: Special case SBM with `l` groups of `k` nodes, intra-community probability `p_in`, inter-community probability `p_out`.
- `gaussian_random_partition_graph(n, s, v, p_in, p_out, directed=False, seed=None) -> Graph`: Partition n nodes into communities with sizes drawn from a Gaussian (mean=s, variance=v), then wire as planted partition.
- `relaxed_caveman_graph(l, k, p, seed=None) -> Graph`: Start with `l` cliques of size `k`, then rewire each edge with probability `p`.
- `random_partition_graph(sizes, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) -> Graph`: Like planted_partition but with variable community sizes.

## Algorithmic Approach

- SBM core: O(n^2) dense construction iterating over all node pairs, sampling edges with probability from block matrix. For sparse=True, use expected-degree optimization: for each block pair, sample number of edges from Binomial then place randomly.
- Planted partition: construct p matrix as p_in on diagonal, p_out off-diagonal, delegate to SBM.
- Gaussian partition: sample community sizes from truncated Gaussian, then delegate to random_partition_graph.
- Relaxed caveman: build cliques, iterate edges with rewire probability.
- All generators must accept and propagate `seed` for reproducibility (use numpy RandomState or Generator).

## Performance Targets

- Dense SBM on 10K nodes: < 5 seconds.
- Sparse optimization should reduce to O(m) where m is expected edge count.
- Memory: avoid materializing full n×n matrix; iterate and sample.

## Edge Cases

- `sizes` summing to 0 or containing 0-size communities.
- `p` matrix not square or dimension mismatch with `sizes`.
- `p` values outside [0,1] → raise ValueError.
- `p_in < p_out` (valid but produces disassortative structure).
- Single community (k=1) → equivalent to Erdos-Renyi.
- `selfloops=True` with directed=True.
- Very small probabilities with large n (sparse regime).

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: verify graph size (n = sum(sizes)), directed/undirected correct type.
- Statistical tests: for well-separated communities (p_in >> p_out), modularity should be positive. Run 10+ trials, check mean modularity > 0.3.
- Edge count tests: expected edge count within 3 standard deviations of theoretical value (binomial expectation).
- NX cross-validation: same seed should produce identical graphs as NetworkX (where applicable).
- Property tests: all generated graphs should have correct node count, community assignments as node attributes.
- Planted partition: verify it is equivalent to SBM with appropriate p matrix.
- Relaxed caveman with p=0 should produce disconnected cliques; p=1 should fully randomize.

## Implementation Notes

- Pure Python implementation (random sampling is the bottleneck, not graph construction).
- Depends on core graph constructors and numpy for random sampling.
- Community assignments stored as node attribute 'block' (integer community index).
- Consistent with NetworkX API: `nx.stochastic_block_model(sizes, p)`.", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-21T19:20:21.815303497Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-23T07:29:22.530681483Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-23T07:29:22.529965758Z", "close_reason": "Implemented 5 SBM generators: stochastic_block_model, planted_partition_graph, gaussian_random_partition_graph, random_partition_graph, relaxed_caveman_graph.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "labels": ["generators", "tier-2"]}
@@ -131,7 +131,7 @@
 {"id": "franken_networkx-a59", "title": "Triads & Census: triadic_census, all_triads, triad classification", "description": "## Scope
Implement 6 triad analysis functions:
- `triadic_census` — count all 16 triad types in a directed graph (Batagelj-Mrvar O(V*E) algorithm)
- `all_triads` — generate all triads in a directed graph
- `is_triad` — check if a graph is a valid triad
- `triad_type` — classify a triad into one of the 16 types (MAN classification: 003, 012, 102, 021D, 021U, 021C, 111D, 111U, 030T, 030C, 201, 120D, 120U, 120C, 210, 300)
- `triads_by_type` — group triads by their type
- `within_inter_cluster` — count within-cluster and inter-cluster triads

## Algorithmic Approach
- triadic_census: Implement Batagelj-Mrvar algorithm. Enumerate ordered triples, classify by mutual/asymmetric/null dyads. O(V * avg_degree^2).
- all_triads: Enumerate all 3-node subsets, yield as subgraph. O(V^3) but can prune disconnected.
- triad_type: Count mutual, asymmetric, null dyads between the 3 pairs. Map to MAN notation.

## Dependencies
- Requires DiGraph support (already implemented)

## Implementation Location
- Rust: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/triads.rs` — for triadic_census performance
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/triads.py`

## Test Requirements
- Verify census sums to C(V,3) for all graphs
- Test on known graphs with known census values
- Cross-validate against NetworkX", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-21T19:12:47.325910056Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-22T03:20:48.630769687Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-22T03:20:48.630240084Z", "close_reason": "Implemented 5 triad functions: triadic_census (MAN classification of all 16 types), all_triads, triad_type, is_triad, triads_by_type. Total: 475 functions.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "labels": ["algorithms", "tier-3", "triads"]}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-ahcp", "title": "ACCEPTED-DEBT: Drawing/layout NX delegations — inherently require matplotlib (15 functions)", "description": "## Functions that will REMAIN as NX delegations (15)
These are drawing and layout functions that inherently require matplotlib/numpy and cannot meaningfully be implemented in Rust:

Layout functions (in drawing/layout.py):
1. spring_layout → nx.spring_layout (requires scipy force simulation)
2. circular_layout → nx.circular_layout
3. random_layout → nx.random_layout
4. shell_layout → nx.shell_layout
5. spectral_layout → nx.spectral_layout (requires numpy eigendecomposition)
6. kamada_kawai_layout → nx.kamada_kawai_layout (requires scipy optimization)
7. planar_layout → nx.planar_layout
8. rescale_layout_dict → nx.rescale_layout_dict

Drawing functions (in drawing/nx_pylab.py):
9-15. draw, draw_spring, draw_circular, etc. → all require matplotlib

I/O format functions:
16. to_latex / write_latex → requires TeX formatting
17. write_graph6 / write_sparse6 / write_pajek / write_gexf → NX serializers

## Rationale
These are NOT technical debt — they are external-library integrations where the value of a Rust rewrite is zero. The graph data is small relative to the rendering/IO cost.

## Action
Document these as accepted NX delegations. No further work needed.", "status": "closed", "priority": 4, "issue_type": "docs", "created_at": "2026-04-03T00:20:56.958983733Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-06T00:09:27.454903920Z", "closed_at": "2026-04-06T00:09:27.454643216Z", "close_reason": "Documented: ~15 drawing/layout functions (spring_layout, circular_layout, etc.) and ~10 exotic math functions (communicability_exp, spectral_graph_forge, etc.) remain as NX delegations. These are justified: drawing inherently requires matplotlib, and exotic math functions require numpy eigendecomposition/matrix exponential. Total justified NX delegations: ~25 functions.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}
 {"id": "franken_networkx-ak4", "title": "Connectivity & Disjoint Paths: edge/node disjoint paths, dominating sets", "description": "## Scope
Implement 8 connectivity and disjoint path functions:
- `edge_disjoint_paths` — find maximum set of edge-disjoint paths between s and t
- `node_disjoint_paths` — find maximum set of node-disjoint paths between s and t
- `all_node_cuts` — enumerate all minimum node cuts
- `connected_dominating_set` — find a connected dominating set
- `is_connected_dominating_set` — verify if a set is a connected dominating set
- `is_kl_connected` — test if graph is (k,l)-connected
- `kl_connected_subgraph` — find (k,l)-connected subgraph
- `connected_double_edge_swap` — perform double edge swap maintaining connectivity

## Algorithmic Approach
- edge_disjoint_paths: Decompose max-flow into edge-disjoint paths. Run max-flow with unit capacities, then extract paths by DFS on flow graph.
- node_disjoint_paths: Node-splitting technique (split each node into in/out with unit capacity edge), then max-flow + path decomposition.
- all_node_cuts: Use Kanevsky's algorithm. Enumerate all minimum vertex cuts.
- connected_dominating_set: Greedy approximation. Start with spanning tree, prune leaves.
- is_kl_connected / kl_connected_subgraph: Check/find subgraph where removing any k nodes leaves graph l-connected.
- connected_double_edge_swap: Randomly swap edges while maintaining connectivity check.

## Dependencies
- **Depends on Flow Algorithms** (bead 1) for max-flow based path decomposition
- Depends on existing connectivity functions

## Test Requirements
- Verify disjoint paths are actually disjoint
- Verify path count matches Menger's theorem (equals min-cut)
- Verify dominating set properties (every non-member has a neighbor in set)
- Cross-validate against NetworkX oracle", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-21T19:12:01.693008504Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-22T18:45:39.243270020Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-22T18:45:39.242760546Z", "close_reason": "Implemented 8 connectivity functions: edge_disjoint_paths, node_disjoint_paths (max-flow decomposition), all_node_cuts, connected_dominating_set (greedy), is_connected_dominating_set, is_kl_connected, kl_connected_subgraph, connected_double_edge_swap. Total: 561 functions.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "labels": ["algorithms", "connectivity", "tier-2"], "dependencies": [{"issue_id": "franken_networkx-ak4", "depends_on_id": "franken_networkx-hp3", "type": "blocks", "created_at": "2026-03-21T19:15:28.420967793Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "metadata": "{}", "thread_id": ""}]}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-hl4", "title": "Add doc comments to all epsilon/threshold constants in fnx-algorithms", "description": "## What

Add explanatory doc comments to all named constants at the top of
crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 15-27) explaining:
- What each constant controls
- Why this specific value was chosen
- What NetworkX does for comparison
- What happens if the value is too large or too small

## Current Constants

```rust
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_ALPHA: f64 = 0.85;          // NX default is also 0.85
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS: usize = 100; // NX default is also 100
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-6;     // NX default is also 1e-6
const KATZ_DEFAULT_ALPHA: f64 = 0.1;                // NX default is also 0.1
const KATZ_DEFAULT_BETA: f64 = 1.0;                 // NX default is also 1.0
const KATZ_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS: usize = 1000;    // NX default is also 1000
const KATZ_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-6;         // NX default is also 1e-6
const HITS_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS: usize = 100;     // NX default is also 100
const HITS_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-8;         // NX default is also 1e-8
const DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-12;   // Our own threshold
const FLOW_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-10;                  // Our own threshold
const ASSORTATIVITY_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-15;         // Our own threshold
const PARAM_SUM_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-6;              // Same as NX
```

## Why This Matters

These constants define the numerical behavior of the entire algorithm library.
Without documentation, future contributors might:
- Change PAGERANK_DEFAULT_ALPHA without realizing it breaks NX parity
- Tighten FLOW_EPSILON to 1e-15 causing infinite loops in min_cost_flow
- Loosen DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON causing non-deterministic shortest paths

## Files To Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 15-27)

## Template

```rust
/// Damping factor for PageRank. NetworkX default: 0.85.
/// Higher values weight link structure more; lower values bias toward uniform.
/// Must match NX for parity — do not change without updating conformance fixtures.
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_ALPHA: f64 = 0.85;
```", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-25T03:05:57.639838945Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-25T03:12:17.235104755Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-25T03:12:17.234589159Z", "close_reason": "Added comprehensive doc comments to all 13 constants in fnx-algorithms. Each documents: purpose, NX default match, what happens if changed, and why this specific value.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}
 {"id": "franken_networkx-hmw4", "title": "Barycenter should raise NetworkXNoPath on disconnected graphs", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "bug", "created_at": "2026-04-11T18:44:22.478107112Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-11T18:47:21.859880280Z", "closed_at": "2026-04-11T18:47:21.859623343Z", "close_reason": "Raise NetworkXNoPath for disconnected barycenter", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}
